ground_truth.title: The Science Behind Losing Weight and Keeping It Off
ground_truth.author: AHW Endowment
ground_truth.main_content: Nearly one-third of Wisconsinites are obese. In a recent Coffee Conversations with Scientists episode, Dr. Tammy Kindel, MD, PhD, bariatric surgeon and associate professor at Froedtert and the Medical College of Wisconsin, explained the science behind losing weight, why sustaining weight loss can be so challenging, and how bariatric surgery helps some patients live healthier lives. Obesity is caused by a person consuming more calories than they expend. Factors such as lifestyle, environmental influences, and genetics also contribute to obesity. It is a risk factor for many serious health conditions including type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, and certain cancers. Losing weight requires adjusting caloric intake and expenditure to create a calorie deficit. According to Dr. Kindel, multiple mechanisms control food intake through signals in the brain and periphery that give signs for hunger and satiety. The body also regulates calorie burn through metabolic rate controlled by hormones, medical conditions, and medications. Many individuals who lose significant weight will regain it within two to three years. Researchers found that even years after weight loss, individuals had not resumed a normal metabolic rate, suggesting sustained diet and exercise routines are necessary to keep weight off. Bariatric surgery reduces stomach size to help reduce food consumption. Patients with BMI 30-35 with an obesity-associated medical condition or BMI 35+ may be candidates. The two most common procedures are Sleeve Gastrectomy and Roux-en-Y Gastric Bypass, which alter signals regarding food intake and can reduce hunger hormones. Key guiding principles include avoiding caloric drinks, tracking diet and habits, and finding enjoyable cardiovascular activity combined with strength training.
with: Nearly one-third of Wisconsinites are obese.
Obesity is caused by a person consuming more calories than they expend.
Losing weight, or body weight regulation, requires adjusting a person's caloric intake and the number of calories they expend.
Many individuals who lose a significant amount of weight will regain that weight within two to three years of losing it.
Bariatric surgery is an approach to weight loss that reduces the size of the stomach, which can help reduce food consumption.
